text: Neil cites Plutarch's account of Harpocrates (Horus child) being born on the winter solstice to Isis, providing comparative evidence from Greco-Roman mythology for understanding nativity narratives within a religious-cultural context beyond Jewish apocalyptic tradition.
quote: Plutarch writes Harpocrates Horus child is born on the winter solstice to Isis 160 years before Soul Invictus

text: Mason explicitly states that monogamy as a standard became established "under Greek and Roman influence" rather than from Judean tradition, showing how Greco-Roman institutional norms shaped evolving practices in this period.
quote: it looks like under Roman influence um Jews living under Roman influence or Greco Roman influence um evolved into this idea of one one husband one wife

text: The Temple Mount itself was Herod's statement of power in Greco-Roman style: "the largest temple and compound in all of the ancient world nothing in Rome Rivals this." Though respecting Jewish law internally, the Temple's overall design and decoration followed Greco-Roman architectural conventions.
quote: when you talk about Jewish art and architecture I'm thinking herod's palace here the different homes the temple it's Jewish but it's probably in the style of Greco-Roman art and architecture

text: The Gospel of Matthew directly adopted the similitudes' imagery of "the son of man sitting on the throne of his glory" to describe Jesus's final judgment role, demonstrating that early Christians drew on pre-existing Greco-Jewish apocalyptic concepts and royal theology rather than developing Christology independently.
quote: the son of man sitting on the throne of his glory is an idea unique to the similitudes" and Matthew's use: "at the renewal of all things when the son of man is Seated on the throne of his glory you who have followed me will also sit on 12 Thrones judging the 12 tribes of Israel

text: Greek mystery religions directly paralleled Christian theological concepts and practices. The Dionysian myth of dismemberment and reconstitution mirrors Christian resurrection theology, while the Eleusinian Mysteries' ritual consumption of "body and blood" directly parallels the Christian Eucharist, suggesting early Christian theology drew explicitly from Greek religious frameworks rather than solely Jewish sources.
quote: Dionysus was torn apart by Titans and his body parts were scattered eventually he was resurrected representing the cycle of death and rebirth" and "during the rites of the elusinian Mysteries initiates would drink psychedelic potion made of wine and beer and would thus consume the bodies of Demeter and Dionysus grain and Vine body and blood

text: The Naassenes incorporated the Attis mystery hymn directly into Jesus theology, explicitly grafting Greek dying-and-rising god language onto Christian resurrection claims.
quote: the nascenes another early christian sect had a hymn to attuse but it was repurposed for jesus...blessed one whether born of pronous zeus or rhea i loudly held thee jesus you the assyrians call thrice desired adonis all egypt calls you osiris

text: The speaker demonstrates that "Son of God" was conferred through divine elevation and political succession, not merely as spiritual status. By comparing Jesus becoming "Son of God" at resurrection (Romans 1:14) to how Augustus was declared "son of god" after Julius Caesar's deification, the transcript shows "Son of God" functioned as a Hellenistic political title tied to power transfer and divine elevation, directly supporting the book's argument about Greco-Christian royal titulature.
quote: according to the roman propaganda that star wasn't just a star to represent caesar that star was caesar that was his soul ascending to heaven...and if you go about 150 or so years later uh you find another figure uh named uh antinous...a new star in the sky that's that's uh intinus's soul rising to heaven and he's become a god...that star was said to actually be the soul of intinus

text: John Hamer discusses Philo's foundational influence on Christian theology, particularly the pre-Christian development of "Son of God," logos, and trinitarian concepts within Hellenized Judaism. This directly supports the book's argument that Greek theological frameworks shaped Christian titles and theology.
quote: philo when i when i first started digging into christian origins there was all these things that i just didn't understand how why are these jews talking about god being a trinity what is all this where's all these ideas coming then i find finally someone put me on the philo i started reading files okay this makes sense now these ideas were already around and certainly we can say that christianity as we as it now exists comes out of a very hellenized judaism

text: Philo's pre-Christian theology already contained Greek philosophical concepts (trinity, logos) that would later characterize Christian theology, demonstrating Greek intellectual frameworks pervading early Jewish thought in Alexandria before Christianity emerged.
quote: philo has all these ideas floating around about god being a trinity a god being of nature of three the logos being the the divine air of all things like what that sounds like christianity right there

text: The speaker demonstrates that trinitarian theological concepts—a God of dual nature mediated by a third spiritual principle—existed in pre-Christian Greek philosophy, particularly Plato's *Timaeus*. This supports the book's argument that "Son of God" and Christological theology should be understood within Greco-Christian rather than purely Jewish frameworks.
quote: the idea of a trinity usually a god of dual nature connected by a third spiritual nature that mediates in between them we see this long before Christianity comes along for example Plato in his Timaeus says...there has to be some bond to mediate between the two of them

text: Derek Bennett discusses Antinous, the lover of Hadrian (2nd century CE), who was "clothed like Osiris" and became "osiramis the reborn and everlasting." Others could be initiated into his cult to become immortalized. This provides a real historical example of deification of an actual person within the Greco-Roman world.
quote: he is clothed like Osiris uh to say that he's he he becomes known as osiramis the reborn and everlasting... others can be initiated into the cult of ananus uh so that they can become immortalized figures like him
